elements.sent-2411-001: We prove that the principal minors of the distance matrix of a tree satisfy a combinatorial expression involving counts of rooted spanning forests of the underlying tree.
elements.sent-2411-002: This generalizes a result of Graham and Pollak.
elements.sent-2411-003: We also give such an expression for the case of trees with edge lengths.
elements.sent-2411-004: We use arguments motivated by potential theory on graphs.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our formulas can be expressed in terms of evaluations of Symanzik polynomials.
elements.sent-2411-006: Let $D$ denote the {\em distance matrix of $G$, defined by setting the $(u,v)$-entry to the length of the unique path from $u$ to $v$.}
elements.sent-2411-007: In~\cite{graham-pollak , Graham and Pollak prove \begin{equation}\label{eq:full-det} \det D = (-1)^{n-1} 2^{n-2} (n-1).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-008: This identity is remarkable in that the result does not depend on the tree structure, beyond the number of vertices.
elements.sent-2411-009: The identity \eqref{eq:full-det was motivated by a problem in data communication, and inspired much further research on distance matrices.}
elements.sent-2411-010: The main result of this paper is to generalize \eqref{eq:full-det by replacing $\det D$ with any of its principal minors.}
elements.sent-2411-011: For a subset $S \subseteq V$, let $D[S]$ denote the submatrix consisting of the $S$-indexed rows and columns of $D$.
elements.sent-2411-012: Let $S \subseteq V$ be a nonempty subset of vertices.
elements.sent-2411-013: Then \begin{equation}\label{eq:main} \det D[S] = (-1)^{|S|-1} 2^{|S|-2} \Big( (n-1)\, \kappa(G;S) - \!\!\! \sum_{F \in \forests_2(G;S)} \left(\outdeg(F,*) - 2\right)^2 \Big),\end{equation} where $\kappa(G;S)$ is the number of $S$-rooted spanning forests of $G$, $\forests_2(G;S)$ is the set of $(S,*)$-rooted spanning forests of $G$, and $\outdeg(F,*)$ denotes the outdegree of the floating component of $F$.
elements.sent-2411-014: When $S = V$ is the full vertex set, the set of $V$-rooted spanning forests is a singleton (consisting of the subgraph with no edges) so $\kappa(G; V) = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-015: Moreover, the set $\forests_2(G; V)$ of $(V, *)$-rooted spanning forests is empty.
elements.sent-2411-016: Thus Theorem~\ref{thm:main recovers the Graham--Pollak identity \eqref{eq:full-det .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Assume the tree $G$ is endowed with a collection of positive real edge lengths $\alpha = \{\alpha_e \colon e\in E\}$.
elements.sent-2411-018: The {\em distance matrix (with respect to $\alpha$), which we will again denote by $\Da$, is defined by setting the $(u,v)$-entry to the sum of the edge lengths $\alpha_e$ along the unique path from $u$ to $v$.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The relation \eqref{eq:full-det has a generalization for the distance matrix of a tree with edge lengths, proved by Bapat, Kirkland, and Neumann (\cite{bapat-kirkland-neumann *{Corollary 2.5 ): \begin{equation}\label{eq:w-full-det} \det \Da = (-1)^{n-1} 2^{n-2} \Big(\sum_{e \in E} \alpha_e\Big) \Big( \prod_{e \in E} \alpha_e \Big).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: We, in fact, prove the following more general result.
elements.sent-2411-021: For an edge subset $A \subseteq E$ let $w(A) = \prod_{e \not\in A \alpha_e$.}
elements.sent-2411-022: For any nonempty subset $S \subseteq V$, we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:w-main} \det \Da[S] = (-1)^{|S|-1} 2^{|S|-2} \Bigg( \sum_{e \in E}\alpha_e \!\!\!\! \sum_{T \in \trees(G;S)} \!\! w(T) - \!\!\!\!\sum_{F \in \forests_2(G;S)} \!\!w(F)\, (\outdeg(F,*) - 2)^2 \!\Bigg),\end{equation} where $\trees(G;S)$ is the set of $S$-rooted spanning forests of $G$, $\forests_2(G;S)$ is the set of $(S,*)$-rooted spanning forests of $G$, $w(T)$ and $w(F)$ denote the weights of the forests $T$ and $F$, and $\outdeg(F, *)$ is the outdegree of the floating component of $F$, as above.
elements.sent-2411-023: Let $S=\{1,2,3,4,5\}$ be the set of leaf vertices.
elements.sent-2411-024: The corresponding distance submatrix is \begin{equation}D[S] =  0 2 3 4 4 2 0 3 4 4 3 3 0 3 3 4 4 3 0 2 4 4 3 2 0 ,\end{equation} whose determinant is $864$.
elements.sent-2411-025: The tree $G$ has $7$ edges and $21$ $S$-rooted spanning forests.
elements.sent-2411-027: Of the floating components in these forests, $14$ have outdegree three, $4$ have outdegree four, and $1$ has outdegree five.
elements.sent-2411-029: One may ask how the expressions $\det D[S]$ vary, as we fix a tree distance matrix and vary the vertex subset $S$.
elements.sent-2411-030: To our knowledge there is no nice behavior among these determinants but, as $S$ varies there is nice behavior of a ``normalized'' version which we describe here.
elements.sent-2411-031: Given a matrix $A$, let $\cof A$ denote the {\em sum of cofactors of $A$, i.e. \begin{equation}\label{eq:cof} \cof A = \sum_{i = 1}^{|S|} \sum_{j = 1}^{|S|} (-1)^{i + j} \det A_{i,j}\end{equation} where $A_{i,j}$ is the submatrix of $A$ that removes the $i$-th row and the $j$-th column.}
elements.sent-2411-032: If $A$ is invertible, then $\cof A$ is the sum of entries of the matrix inverse $A^{-1}$ multiplied by a factor of $\det A$, i.e. $\cof A = (\det A) (\bone^\tr A^{-1 \bone)$.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In \cite{bapat-sivasubramanian , Bapat and Sivasubramanian show the following identity for the sum of cofactors of a distance submatrix $D[S]$ of a tree with edge lengths $\{\alpha_e \colon e \in E\}$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:cof-trees} \cof D[S] = (-2)^{|S| - 1} \!\!\!\sum_{T \in \trees(G;S)} w(T) ,\end{equation} where $\trees(G;S)$ denotes the set of $S$-rooted spanning forests of $G$ (see \S\ref{sec:graphs-matrices and Theorem~\ref{thm:distance-sub-cof ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: An immediate consequence of Theorem~\ref{thm:w-main , together with \eqref{eq:cof-trees , is the identity \begin{equation}\label{eq:det-cof} \frac{\det D[S]}{\cof D[S]} = \frac12 \left( \sum_{e \in E} \alpha_e - \frac{\sum_{F \in \forests_2(G; S)} w(F) \,(\outdeg(F,*) - 2)^2}{\sum_{T \in \trees(G; S)} w(T)} \right).\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}\label{eq:det-cof} \frac{\det D[S]}{\cof D[S]} = \frac12 \left( \sum_{e \in E} \alpha_e - \frac{\sum_{F \in \forests_2(G; S)} w(F) \,(\outdeg(F,*) - 2)^2}{\sum_{T \in \trees(G; S)} w(T)} \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-035: We will refer to ${\det D[S] /{\cof D[S]}$ as the {\em normalized principal minor corresponding to the vertex subset $S$.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: It turns out that normalized principal minors satisfy a monotonicity condition.
elements.sent-2411-037: This result is obtained using the method of Lagrange multipliers, and relies on knowledge of the signature of $D[S]$.

elements.sent-2411-042: For a nonempty subset $S \subseteq V$, \begin{equation}\frac{\det \Da[S]}{\cof \Da[S]} \leq \frac12 \sum_{e \in E(\conv(S, G))} \alpha_e .\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-048: Recall, given a graph $G = (V, E)$, the {\em first Symanzik polynomial is the homogeneous polynomial in edge-indexed variables $x = \{x_e \colon e \in E\}$ defined by \begin{equation}\psi_G(x) = \sum_{T \in \trees(G)} \prod_{e \not \in T} x_e ,\end{equation} where $\trees(G)$ denotes the set of spanning trees of $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: A ``momentum function'' is any $p\colon V \to \RR$ satisfying the constraint $\sum_{v \in V p(v) = 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-050: The {\em second Symanzik polynomial is \begin{equation}\varphi_G(p; x) = \sum_{F \in \forests_2(G)} \Big(\sum_{v \in F_1} p(v) \Big)^2 \prod_{e \not\in F} x_e ,\end{equation} where $\forests_2(G)$ is the set of two-component spanning forests of $G$ and $F_1$ denotes one of the components of $F = F_1 \cup F_2$.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Note that it does not matter which component we label as $F_1$, since the momentum constraint implies that $\sum_{v \in F_1 p(v) = - \sum_{v \in F_2 p(v)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: We note that the expressions in Theorem~\ref{thm:w-main and \eqref{eq:det-cof are closely related to Symanzik polynomials.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Let $\psi_{(G/S)}$ and $\varphi_{(G/S)}$ denote the first and second Symanzik polynomials of the quotient graph $G/S$.
elements.sent-2411-054: Let $p_{\rm can}$ be the momentum function defined on $G/S$ by $p_{\rm can (v) = \deg(v) - 2$ for $v \not \in S$ and $p_{\rm can (v_S) = -\sum_{v \not\in S p_{\rm can (v)$, where $v_S$ denotes the vertex image of $S$ in the quotient.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The identity in Theorem~\ref{thm:w-main can be written as follows \begin{equation}\label{eq:det-symanzik} \det D[S] = (-1)^{|S|-1} 2^{|S|-2} \left( \Big(\sum_{e \in E}\alpha_e \Big)\, \psi_{(G/S)}(\alpha) - \phi_{(G/S)}(p_{\rm can}; \alpha) \right) .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-056: The ``normalized'' ratio \eqref{eq:det-cof can be written using Symanzik polynomials as \begin{equation}\label{eq:det-cof-symanzik} \frac{\det D[S]}{\cof D[S]} = \frac12 \left( \sum_{e \in E} \alpha_e - \frac{\varphi_{(G/S)}(p_{\rm can}; \alpha)}{\psi_{(G/S)}(\alpha)} \right) .\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-067: On a tree, effective resistance is the same as (path-)distance.
elements.sent-2411-068: If $D$ is the distance matrix of a tree, then any principal minor $D[S]$ is the effective resistance matrix of some (edge-weighted) graph on the vertex set $S$.
elements.sent-2411-069: The quantity $(\det D[S] / \cof D[S])$ is referred to as the {\em resistance radius of $S$ (up to a factor of $2$), and the vector $\boldm$ used in \S\ref{sec:distance_proofs is called the {\em resistance curvature (up to scaling) in Devriendt's terminology.}}}

elements.sent-2411-077: The results in this paper may be of interest to those studying phylogenetics.
elements.sent-2411-078: In phylogenetics, one aims to find the tree that best represents the evolutionary history among a collection of organisms using biological data (e.g. DNA sequences).
elements.sent-2411-079: In this tree, leaf vertices represent modern-day species, while internal vertices represent ancestral species.
elements.sent-2411-080: There are standard methods for estimating pairwise distances between species along their evolutionary tree.
elements.sent-2411-081: This means we can often predict the distance submatrix $D[S]$ of the target tree, in which $S$ is the set of leaf vertices, and we would like to use this information in reverse to decide what underlying tree best fits this distance data.
elements.sent-2411-082: Results such as Theorem~\ref{thm:monotonic may lead to new tests for phylogenetic inference, or for evaluating tree instability~\cite{collienne-etal .}}

elements.sent-2411-100: A {\em spanning tree of a graph $G$ is a subgraph which is connected, has no cycles, and contains all vertices of $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-101: A {\em spanning forest of a graph $G$ is a subgraph which has no cycles and contains all vertices of $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-102: Given a set of vertices $S = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_r\}$, an {\em $S$-rooted spanning forest of $G$ is a spanning forest which has exactly one vertex of $S$ in each connected component.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Given $s \in S$ and a forest $F$, we let $F(s)$ denote the {\em $s$-component of $F$, that is, the component of $F$ containing $s$.}
elements.sent-2411-104: An {\em $(S,*)$-rooted spanning forest of $G$ is a spanning forest which has $|S|+1$ components, where $|S|$ components each contain one vertex of $S$, and the additional component is disjoint from $S$.}
elements.sent-2411-105: We call the component disjoint from $S$ the {\em floating component , following terminology in \cite{kassel-kenyon-wu .}}
elements.sent-2411-106: For an $(S,*)$-rooted spanning forest $F$, we let $F(*)$ denote the floating component.
elements.sent-2411-107: We sometimes refer to the floating component as the $*$-component of $F$.
elements.sent-2411-108: Again, for $s \in S$, we let $F(s)$ denote the $s$-component of $F$.
elements.sent-2411-109: Let $\trees(G;S)$ denote the set of $S$-rooted spanning forests of $G$, and let $\forests_2(G;S)$ denote the set of $(S,*)$-rooted spanning forests of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-110: Then $\trees(G;S)$ contains $11$ forests, while $\forests_2(G;S)$ contains $19$ forests.
elements.sent-2411-111: Some of these are shown in Figure~\ref{fig:1-forests and Figure~\ref{fig:2-forests , respectively.}}
